summary.author: Derek Melchin

summary.description_full:
The strategy is a volatility mean reversion overlay that trades short dated SPX option straddles as a function of how extreme spot VIX is versus its recent history. Each day it computes a daily VIX z score using approximately two years of daily data (about 504 trading days) via a rolling mean and standard deviation. The core thesis is that unusually high implied volatility (high positive z score) tends to revert and is monetizable via short straddle premium capture, while unusually low implied volatility (negative z score) implies poor carry for short volatility and can justify taking the opposite exposure.

The portfolio expresses this view using the nearest expiring SPX at the money straddle (call plus put at the closest strike to spot) selected from the option chain filtered to roughly 30 day straddles, with decisions made once per day about 30 minutes after the market opens. Position size is discrete and proportional to signal strength: it targets a number of straddles equal to the negative integer of the VIX z score, so higher VIX z scores increase short straddle exposure; if the z score rounds to zero, all positions are liquidated. The primary risk control is a flat position regime when the signal is weak and forced liquidation on any option assignment event. The intended alpha source is volatility risk premium capture conditioned on volatility regime (z score) rather than constant short vol.

raw.name: VIX Z-Score Straddle Mean Reversion

author.name: Derek Melchin
author.bio:
Quantitative Developer at QuantConnect. Studied Computer Science and Finance at the University of Lethbridge. Competitor in the 2020-1 Rotman International Trading Competitions. 
See my latest posts at derekmelchin.com.
